passages.explanation: Jesus calls the Twelve together and gives them power and authority to drive out all demons and to cure diseases. He sends them out to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal the sick. He tells them to take nothing for the journey — no staff, no bag, no bread, no money, no extra shirt. Whatever house they enter, stay there until they leave that town. If people don't welcome them, shake the dust off their feet as a testimony against them. They set out, going from village to village, proclaiming the good news and healing people everywhere. The instructions enforce dependence — no provisions means relying on God and on the hospitality of the people they serve. Ministry without a safety net.

Herod the tetrarch hears about everything going on and is perplexed. Some say John has been raised from the dead, others that Elijah has appeared, others that one of the prophets of long ago has come back. Herod says: I beheaded John — so who is this I hear such things about? And he tries to see Jesus. Herod's curiosity is political, not spiritual. He's unsettled because he can't categorise Jesus, and an uncontrolled religious movement threatens stability.

The apostles return and report what they've done. Jesus takes them with him and they withdraw to a town called Bethsaida. But the crowds learn where he is and follow. He welcomes them, speaks about the kingdom of God, and heals those who need healing. Late in the afternoon the Twelve come to him: send the crowd away so they can find food and lodging. Jesus says: you give them something to eat. They have only five loaves and two fish — unless they go and buy food for all these people (about five thousand men). Jesus tells the disciples to have the people sit down in groups of about fifty. He takes the five loaves and two fish, looks up to heaven, gives thanks, breaks them, and gives them to the disciples to distribute. Everyone eats and is satisfied. Twelve baskets of leftovers are collected — one for each apostle. Jesus told them to feed the crowd, and then showed them how. The twelve baskets make the point personally: there's more than enough, even for the ones serving.

Once when Jesus is praying in private with his disciples nearby, he asks them: who do the crowds say I am? They reply: John the Baptist; others say Elijah; still others that one of the prophets of long ago has come back to life. He asks: but what about you — who do you say I am? Peter answers: God's Messiah. Jesus strictly warns them not to tell anyone. Then he says: the Son of Man must suffer many things, be rejected by the elders, chief priests, and teachers of the law, be killed, and on the third day be raised to life. Peter's confession is correct but incomplete — he doesn't yet understand what kind of Messiah Jesus will be. Jesus immediately connects messiahship to suffering, rejection, and death. The glory comes through the cross, not around it.

Jesus says to them all: whoever wants to be my disciple must deny themselves, take up their cross daily, and follow me. Whoever wants to save their life will lose it, but whoever loses their life for my sake will save it. What good is it to gain the whole world and yet lose or forfeit yourself? Whoever is ashamed of me and my words, the Son of Man will be ashamed of them when he comes in his glory and the glory of the Father and the holy angels. Truly I tell you, some standing here will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God. The cross isn't a metaphor for inconvenience — in the Roman world it means execution. Jesus is saying: following me may cost you everything, and that's still the better deal.

About eight days after saying this, Jesus takes Peter, John, and James up a mountain to pray. As he prays, the appearance of his face changes and his clothes become as bright as a flash of lightning. Two men appear in glory — Moses and Elijah — talking with Jesus about his departure, which he's about to bring to fulfilment in Jerusalem. Peter and the others are very sleepy, but when they become fully awake, they see his glory and the two men standing with him. As Moses and Elijah are leaving, Peter says: master, it's good for us to be here. Let us put up three shelters — one for you, one for Moses, one for Elijah. He doesn't know what he's saying. A cloud appears and covers them, and they're afraid. A voice from the cloud says: this is my Son, whom I have chosen — listen to him. When the voice finishes, Jesus is found alone. The disciples keep this to themselves and tell no one at that time. Moses represents the Law, Elijah the Prophets — and they both point to Jesus. The Father's command is simple: listen to him. Not to Moses or Elijah — to him.

The next day, when they come down from the mountain, a large crowd meets them. A man calls out: teacher, I beg you to look at my son — he's my only child. A spirit seizes him, he screams, it throws him into convulsions and he foams at the mouth. It scarcely ever leaves him and is destroying him. I begged your disciples to drive it out, but they couldn't. Jesus responds: you unbelieving and perverse generation, how long shall I stay with you and put up with you? Bring your son here. As the boy is coming, the demon throws him to the ground in a convulsion. Jesus rebukes the impure spirit, heals the boy, and gives him back to his father. Everyone is amazed at God's greatness. While they're all marvelling, Jesus tells his disciples: listen carefully — the Son of Man is going to be delivered into the hands of men. They don't understand and are afraid to ask. The contrast between mountain glory and valley failure is deliberate. And right when everyone is impressed, Jesus redirects to the cross again.

An argument starts among the disciples about which of them is the greatest. Jesus, knowing their thoughts, takes a little child and has the child stand beside him. He says: whoever welcomes this little child in my name welcomes me, and whoever welcomes me welcomes the one who sent me. For it's the one who is least among you all who is the greatest. John says: master, we saw someone driving out demons in your name and we tried to stop him, because he's not one of us. Jesus replies: don't stop him — whoever is not against you is for you. The disciples are arguing about rank while Jesus is talking about death. Then they try to gatekeep who's allowed to do ministry. Jesus corrects both impulses: greatness is service, and the kingdom is bigger than your group.

As the time approaches for Jesus to be taken up to heaven, he resolutely sets out for Jerusalem. He sends messengers ahead to a Samaritan village to prepare for him. But the people there don't welcome him because he's heading for Jerusalem. When James and John see this, they ask: Lord, do you want us to call fire down from heaven to destroy them? Jesus turns and rebukes them. They go to another village. The disciples want to play Elijah and destroy the Samaritans. Jesus rebukes the impulse. His face is set toward Jerusalem — toward his own death — and the disciples want to kill people who are rude to them. The irony is heavy. Luke later records in Acts that these same apostles will return to preach the gospel to Samaritans.

As they're walking along the road, someone says: I will follow you wherever you go. Jesus replies: foxes have dens and birds have nests, but the Son of Man has no place to lay his head. He says to another man: follow me. The man replies: Lord, first let me go and bury my father. Jesus says: let the dead bury their own dead — you go and proclaim the kingdom of God. Still another says: I will follow you, Lord, but first let me go back and say goodbye to my family. Jesus replies: no one who puts a hand to the plough and looks back is fit for service in the kingdom of God. Three potential followers, three excuses — comfort, family duty, emotional attachment. Jesus isn't being cruel; he's being clear. Following him requires an immediate, total reorientation of priorities. Half-commitments don't survive the road to Jerusalem.
